title: Control of relaxation properties of a macroscopic nuclear spin ensemble
authors: János Ádám, Andrew J. Winter, Deniz Aybas, Dmitry Budker, Derek F. Jackson Kimball, Arne Wickenbrock, Alexander O. Sushkov
abstract: Macroscopic spin ensembles in solids are powerful platforms for quantum sensing and precision metrology. A key challenge is controlling the nuclear spin population relaxation time $T_1$, which can become prohibitively long at cryogenic temperatures due to phonon freeze-out. We demonstrate optical control of the $T_1$ relaxation time of the $^{207}$Pb nuclear spin ensemble in lead-containing ferroelectric crystals PbTiO$_3$ (PT) and (PbMg$_{1/3}$Nb$_{2/3}$O$_3$)$_{2/3}$-(PbTiO$_3$)$_{1/3}$ (PMN-PT). Using X-band electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy at 10 K, we characterize light-induced paramagnetic centers created by 405 nm laser illumination. In PT, we observe paramagnetic Pb$^{3+}$ centers and their hyperfine interaction with nearby nuclear spins. In PMN-PT, we identify two populations: isotropic Pb$^{3+}$ centers and anisotropic Ti$^{3+}$ centers occupying $d$-orbitals, with spin number densities of $(2.5 \pm 1.0) \times 10^{17}$ cm$^{-3}$ and $(4.1 \pm 1.7) \times 10^{17}$ cm$^{-3}$, respectively. Power-dependent EPR measurements enable extraction of spin relaxation times. We investigate the ionization and recombination dynamics of these transient paramagnetic centers. Using saturation-recovery nuclear magnetic resonance, we demonstrate that laser illumination reduces the $^{207}$Pb nuclear $T_1$ by approximately a factor of two, from $(17 \pm 2)$ s to $(7 \pm 1)$ s at 4.6 MHz, and from $(1550 \pm 40)$ s to $(850 \pm 70)$ s at 40 MHz. We develop a model relating the nuclear relaxation rate to the density of photoinduced paramagnetic centers. This optical control of nuclear spin relaxation provides a pathway toward accelerated thermal polarization and dynamic nuclear polarization in solid-state NMR-based precision measurements, including searches for axion-like dark matter.
summary: This paper demonstrates a method to optically control the nuclear spin relaxation time in lead-based ferroelectric crystals. By using a 405 nm laser, the researchers induce paramagnetic centers that act as a spin bath, effectively shortening the long T1 times caused by cryogenic temperatures. This technique allows for faster thermal polarization and dynamic nuclear polarization. Such control is a critical step toward improving the sensitivity of NMR-based searches for dark matter, such as axion-like particles.
main_problem: Controlling the nuclear spin population relaxation time (T1) in macroscopic spin ensembles at cryogenic temperatures, where phonon freeze-out leads to prohibitively long relaxation times.
main_result: Demonstrated that 405 nm laser illumination can optically control the 207Pb nuclear T1 relaxation time by creating transient paramagnetic centers (Pb3+ and Ti3+) in ferroelectric crystals.
method: Used X-band Electron Paramagnetic Resonance (EPR) spectroscopy and saturation-recovery Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) at 10 K under 405 nm laser illumination.
model_or_system: The system consists of 207Pb nuclear spin ensembles in lead-containing ferroelectric crystals (PbTiO3 and PMN-PT). The model relates the nuclear relaxation rate to the density of photoinduced paramagnetic centers using a Zeeman and hyperfine interaction Hamiltonian.
key_observables: EPR spectra (voltage signal), g-factors, linewidths, spin number densities, and nuclear T1 relaxation times.
important_parameters: Temperature of 10 K; 405 nm laser wavelength; spin densities of approximately 10^17 cm^-3; reduction of T1 from 1550 s to 850 s at 40 MHz.
main_assumptions: Isotropic hyperfine interaction assumption for certain centers; powder-like orientational averaging for Ti3+ due to lattice disorder; treating unresolved hyperfine structure as Gaussian broadening.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the experimental setup; Figure 2 shows the EPR spectrum of PT; Figure 7 shows the spatial dependence of the microwave field along the cavity axis.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of long T1 times, describes the experimental setup and materials, details the EPR characterization of paramagnetic centers, presents NMR results for T1 control, and concludes with a model relating relaxation to spin density.
why_interesting: The ability to optically manipulate relaxation rates in a solid-state spin bath is highly relevant to open quantum systems and the development of efficient initialization protocols for quantum sensing and precision metrology.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Experimental setup schematic. The probe field (blue arrow) generated in the microwave source travels through a circulator and is coupled into a cavity. We place the sample (yellow rectangle) inside a temperature controlled cryostat. The sample is placed in the middle of the cavity (for PMN-PT we observed heavy losses therefore displaced the sample from the cavity center). A 405 nm laser light (purple wavy arrow) is coupled using a quartz rod. A bias magnetic field (B0) is applied. After illumination paramagnetic centers are excited; this changes the impedance of the cavity which results in reflections (red arrow). The reflected signal travels through the circulator and gets absorbed...

FIG. 2. (a) The EPR spectrum of PT crystal at 10 K and 0.2 mW (30 dB attenuation) illuminated by 405 nm laser (blue dots). The signal belongs to the electron spins of 204Pb, 206Pb and 208Pb paramagnetic centers which have no nuclear spin. The central line is associated with the electron spin Zeeman interaction of the I = 0 paramagnetic center, the satellite lines on each side arise from the super-hyperfine interactions of the electron spin with the nuclear spins on neighboring atomic lattice sites. We model the spectrum with the superposition of eleven Lorentzian lineshapes (red solid line). (b) the EPR spectrum of PMN-PT crystal at 10 K and 1.3 mW (22 dB attenuation) illuminated by 405 nm...

FIG. 3. (a) Saturation curve of PT. The spectrum of PT has been measured at different microwave amplitudes and the area under the data has been integrated (green dots). The data has been fitted (dark green line) with Eq. (6) where a = 2.3 × 104, b = 2 × 104. The signal saturates at high amplitudes due to power broadening. The linear equation, without the power broadening denominator term is shown with light green dashed line. (b) Saturation curve of PMNPT. The spectrum of PMN- PT has been measured at different microwave powers, similar to (a). However, since the spectrum is the superposition of two different populations, direct integration is not possible. Therefore the fitted curves has...

FIG. 4. The photo-ionization and recombination dynamics of light-induced paramagnetic centers. (a) Build-up and decay of paramagnetic centers in PT at 10 K. The purple shaded area shows when the laser was on. (b) The decay of densities of Pb3+

FIG. 5. Pb NMR saturation-recovery measurement of PMN-PT with light off (blue) and light on (red). The Y-axis shows transverse magnetization, normalized in units of maximal longitudinal magnetization. The X-axis shows the saturation recovery delay time τ. (a) The measurements at 40 MHz NMR frequency. (b) The measurements at 4.6 MHz NMR frequency.
FIG. 6. The power lineshape with B⊥= 3510 G and B∥= 3540 G.
